topics.meaning: Every composite number can be expressed as a product of primes, and this factorisation is unique apart from the order of the factors.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic states that every composite number can be factorised as a product of prime numbers, and this prime factorisation is unique except for the order in which the primes appear. For example, 32760 = 2^3 x 3^2 x 5 x 7 x 13 and no other set of primes can produce this number. This is why prime numbers are called the building blocks of all natural numbers. The theorem is proved by first showing every number can be written as a product of primes, then showing this representation is unique. It has wide applications, such as finding HCF and LCM and proving numbers irrational. In general, if a number N = p1^a1 x p2^a2 x ... x pk^ak, this representation is unique.
topics.easyExplanation: Every composite number breaks down into prime factors in exactly one way (ignoring order). For example, 12 = 2 x 2 x 3 always.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Pick a composite number
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Take any composite number, say 156, that you want to factorise.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Divide by smallest prime
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Keep dividing by the smallest possible prime: 156 = 2 x 78 = 2 x 2 x 39.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Continue factorising
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Continue until only primes remain: 39 = 3 x 13, so 156 = 2^2 x 3 x 13.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Write in power form
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Express repeated primes using exponents: 156 = 2^2 x 3 x 13.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Confirm uniqueness
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: No other combination of primes multiplies to 156, showing the factorisation is unique.

importantKeywords.definition: A natural number greater than 1 having exactly two factors, 1 and itself.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: 7 is prime because only 1 and 7 divide it.

importantKeywords.definition: A natural number greater than 1 that has more than two factors.
Expressing a number as a product of its prime factors.

topics.quickSummary: The Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic guarantees that every composite number has a unique prime factorisation apart from order. This makes primes the fundamental building blocks of natural numbers and underlies methods for HCF, LCM and irrationality proofs.
revisionNotes: Every composite number = product of primes, uniquely.
Order of primes does not matter.
Use repeated division by smallest prime.
Basis for HCF, LCM and irrationality proofs.

topics.topic: HCF and LCM using Prime Factorisation
topics.meaning: The HCF is the product of the smallest powers of common prime factors, while the LCM is the product of the greatest powers of all prime factors involved.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Using the Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic, the HCF (Highest Common Factor) of two numbers is found by taking the product of the smallest power of each common prime factor, and the LCM (Lowest Common Multiple) is the product of the greatest power of each prime factor appearing in the numbers. For example, for 6 = 2 x 3 and 20 = 2^2 x 5, HCF = 2^1 = 2 and LCM = 2^2 x 3 x 5 = 60. An important relationship holds for any two positive integers a and b: HCF(a,b) x LCM(a,b) = a x b. This formula allows finding one quantity if the other three are known. Note this product relationship is valid only for two numbers, not for three or more.
topics.easyExplanation: Write both numbers as products of primes. HCF takes the lowest powers of common primes; LCM takes the highest powers of all primes. Also HCF x LCM = product of the two numbers.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Prime factorise both numbers
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Express each number as a product of primes, e.g. 96 = 2^5 x 3 and 404 = 2^2 x 101.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Identify common primes
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Find primes common to both: here only 2 is common.

stepByStepExplanation.title: HCF = lowest powers
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Take the smallest power of common primes: HCF = 2^2 = 4.

stepByStepExplanation.title: LCM = highest powers
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Take the greatest power of every prime: LCM = 2^5 x 3 x 101 = 9696.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Verify
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Check HCF x LCM = product: 4 x 9696 = 38784 = 96 x 404.

examples: Find HCF and LCM of 6 and 20: 6 = 2 x 3, 20 = 2^2 x 5. HCF = 2, LCM = 2^2 x 3 x 5 = 60. Check: 2 x 60 = 120 = 6 x 20.
Given HCF(a,b) = 9 and LCM = 360 with a = 45, find b: b = (HCF x LCM)/a = (9 x 360)/45 = 72.

importantKeywords.definition: The largest number that divides two or more numbers exactly.
The smallest number that is a multiple of two or more numbers.
A factor shared by two or more numbers.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: 3 is a common factor of 9 and 15.

topics.quickSummary: HCF and LCM can be found from prime factorisations: HCF uses lowest common powers, LCM uses highest powers. For two numbers, HCF x LCM = product of the numbers, a relation useful for quick calculations.
revisionNotes: HCF = product of lowest powers of common primes.
LCM = product of highest powers of all primes.
HCF x LCM = a x b (two numbers only).
Prime factorise first, then compare powers.

topics.meaning: An irrational number is a real number that cannot be written in the form p/q where p and q are integers and q is not zero.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: A number is called irrational if it cannot be expressed as p/q where p and q are integers and q is not equal to 0. Examples include sqrt(2), sqrt(3), sqrt(5) and pi. Using the Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic, we can prove such numbers are irrational. A key supporting theorem states: if a prime p divides a^2, then p divides a (for a positive integer a). The proof of irrationality uses contradiction: we assume the number is rational, derive a contradiction about common factors, and conclude our assumption was wrong. The real number system is made up of rational numbers together with irrational numbers.
topics.easyExplanation: Irrational numbers cannot be written as a simple fraction p/q. Numbers like sqrt(2) and pi are irrational and have non-terminating non-repeating decimals.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Assume rational
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: To prove sqrt(2) irrational, assume sqrt(2) = a/b where a and b are coprime integers, b not 0.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Square both sides
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Then 2 = a^2/b^2, so a^2 = 2b^2, meaning 2 divides a^2.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Apply prime theorem
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Since 2 divides a^2, 2 divides a, so write a = 2c.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Substitute back
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Then 2b^2 = 4c^2, so b^2 = 2c^2, meaning 2 divides b as well.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Reach contradiction
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Both a and b are divisible by 2, contradicting that they are coprime; hence sqrt(2) is irrational.

examples: Prove sqrt(2) is irrational: assuming sqrt(2) = a/b coprime leads to both a and b being even, a contradiction, so sqrt(2) is irrational.
Show 3 + 2 sqrt(5) is irrational: if it were rational r, then sqrt(5) = (r-3)/2 would be rational, contradicting that sqrt(5) is irrational.

importantKeywords.definition: A real number not expressible as p/q with integers p, q and q not 0.
A number expressible as p/q with integers p, q and q not 0.
Assuming the opposite of what you want, then deriving an impossibility.

topics.quickSummary: Irrational numbers cannot be written as p/q and have non-terminating non-repeating decimals. Using the theorem that a prime dividing a^2 divides a, numbers like sqrt(2), sqrt(3) and sqrt(5) are proven irrational by contradiction.
revisionNotes: Irrational = cannot be written as p/q.
sqrt of a non-perfect-square is irrational.
If prime p divides a^2 then p divides a.
Proof uses contradiction with coprime assumption.

topics.meaning: A rational number has a terminating decimal expansion exactly when its denominator (in lowest terms) has only 2 and 5 as prime factors.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: For a rational number p/q in its lowest form, the nature of its decimal expansion depends only on the prime factorisation of the denominator q. If q can be written in the form 2^n x 5^m (only the primes 2 and 5), then p/q has a terminating decimal expansion. If q has any prime factor other than 2 or 5, then p/q has a non-terminating but repeating (recurring) decimal expansion. For example, 3/8 = 3/2^3 terminates as 0.375, while 1/7 is non-terminating repeating. Conversely, any terminating decimal is rational with denominator of the form 2^n x 5^m. This gives a quick test without actually performing the division.
topics.easyExplanation: Look at the denominator in lowest form. If it has only 2s and 5s, the decimal stops (terminates). Otherwise it repeats forever.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Reduce to lowest terms
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Write the fraction p/q in simplest form, e.g. 13/3125.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Factorise the denominator
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Factorise q into primes: 3125 = 5^5.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Check for 2 and 5 only
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: If q = 2^n x 5^m with no other primes, the decimal terminates.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Decide the type
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Here q = 5^5 so 13/3125 terminates; if a prime like 3 or 7 appeared it would repeat.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Find places if needed
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Number of decimal places equals the larger of n and m; here 5 places: 0.00416.

examples: Determine 17/8: 8 = 2^3, only prime 2, so terminating. 17/8 = 2.125.
Determine 64/455: 455 = 5 x 7 x 13, has primes 7 and 13, so non-terminating repeating.

importantKeywords.definition: A decimal that ends after a finite number of digits.
A decimal that goes on forever with a repeating block.
The bottom number of a fraction.

topics.quickSummary: A rational number p/q in lowest terms terminates if and only if q has only 2 and 5 as prime factors; otherwise it is non-terminating repeating. This lets you classify a decimal expansion just by factorising the denominator.
revisionNotes: Decimal places = max power of 2 or 5.

topics.meaning: Euclid's division lemma states that for positive integers a and b, there exist unique integers q and r such that a = bq + r with 0 less than or equal to r less than b.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Euclid's division lemma formalises the long division process: given two positive integers a and b, there exist unique whole numbers q (quotient) and r (remainder) such that a = b x q + r, where 0 <= r < b. For example, dividing 455 by 42 gives 455 = 42 x 10 + 35. This lemma is the basis of Euclid's division algorithm for computing the HCF of two numbers by repeated division. The remainder is always smaller than the divisor, which guarantees the process eventually ends. Although simple to state, it has many applications in number theory and divisibility.
topics.easyExplanation: When you divide one whole number by another, you always get a quotient and a remainder smaller than the divisor. This is written a = bq + r.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Identify dividend and divisor
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Choose a = 455 (dividend) and b = 42 (divisor).

stepByStepExplanation.title: Find the quotient
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Determine how many times 42 fits into 455: q = 10.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Find the remainder
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Compute r = 455 - 42 x 10 = 35, and check 0 <= 35 < 42.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Write the lemma form
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Express as 455 = 42 x 10 + 35.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Note uniqueness
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: For these a and b, the pair (q, r) = (10, 35) is the only one satisfying the conditions.

examples: Express 96 in terms of dividing by 7: 96 = 7 x 13 + 5, with remainder 5 < 7.

importantKeywords.definition: The number being divided.
The whole number of times the divisor fits.
What is left over, always smaller than the divisor.

topics.quickSummary: Euclid's division lemma says any pair of positive integers a, b gives unique q and r with a = bq + r and 0 <= r < b. It captures ordinary long division and forms the foundation of the division algorithm for HCF.
revisionNotes: a = bq + r with 0 <= r < b.
q is quotient, r is remainder.
Remainder is always less than divisor.
Basis for finding HCF by division.

topics.meaning: Euclid's division algorithm finds the HCF of two positive integers by repeated division until the remainder becomes zero.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Euclid's division algorithm is a step-by-step method to find the HCF of two positive integers using the division lemma repeatedly. To find HCF of a and b (with a > b), apply the lemma a = bq + r. If r = 0, the HCF is b. If r is not 0, replace a by b and b by r, and repeat. The process continues until the remainder becomes zero; the divisor at that stage is the required HCF. This works because the HCF of a and b equals the HCF of b and r at each step. The method is efficient and avoids prime factorisation.
topics.easyExplanation: Keep dividing: divide the bigger number by the smaller, then divide the divisor by the remainder, and repeat until remainder is 0. The last divisor is the HCF.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Check the remainder
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Remainder 35 is not 0, so continue.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Shift and repeat
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Now divide 42 by 35: 42 = 35 x 1 + 7.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Continue until zero
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Divide 35 by 7: 35 = 7 x 5 + 0; remainder is now 0.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Read off the HCF
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The last divisor when remainder became 0 is 7, so HCF(455, 42) = 7.

importantKeywords.definition: A definite step-by-step procedure to solve a problem.
The greatest number dividing both given numbers.
Repeating a step using new values each time.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Replacing a,b by b,r each round.

topics.quickSummary: Euclid's division algorithm finds the HCF by repeatedly applying a = bq + r, replacing the numbers with divisor and remainder until the remainder is zero. The final divisor is the HCF, an efficient alternative to prime factorisation.
revisionNotes: Replace a,b with b,r until r = 0.
Last non-zero divisor is the HCF.

topics.meaning: HCF always divides LCM, and for two numbers their product equals the product of their HCF and LCM.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Several useful properties connect HCF and LCM. For any two positive integers a and b, the relation HCF(a,b) x LCM(a,b) = a x b holds, so one can be found if the other three are known. The HCF of two numbers always divides their LCM exactly. The HCF is never larger than either number, while the LCM is never smaller than either number. If two numbers are coprime (HCF = 1), then their LCM equals their product. These properties help check answers and solve word problems involving common timings, packaging, and grouping. The product rule applies only to a pair of numbers, not to three or more.
topics.easyExplanation: For two numbers, HCF times LCM equals the two numbers multiplied. The HCF divides the LCM, and coprime numbers have LCM equal to their product.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Recall the product rule
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: For two numbers, HCF x LCM = product of the numbers.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Use to find unknowns
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: If three of HCF, LCM, a, b are known, compute the fourth.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Check divisibility
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Confirm the HCF divides the LCM with no remainder.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Test coprimality
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: If HCF = 1 the numbers are coprime and LCM = a x b.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Apply to problems
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Use these in word problems like finding when two bells ring together (LCM).

examples: If HCF(a,b)=12 and LCM=180, and a=36, then b=(12 x 180)/36=60.
Numbers 8 and 9 are coprime (HCF=1), so LCM = 8 x 9 = 72.

importantKeywords.definition: Two numbers whose only common factor is 1.
HCF x LCM = product of the two numbers.
One number going exactly into another.

topics.quickSummary: Key properties: HCF x LCM = product of two numbers, HCF divides LCM, HCF <= each number <= LCM, and coprime numbers have LCM equal to their product. These relations speed up calculations and solve timing and grouping problems.
revisionNotes: HCF x LCM = a x b (two numbers).

topics.meaning: The concepts of HCF and LCM are applied to real-life problems such as arranging objects in groups and finding when repeating events coincide.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Real number concepts have practical applications. HCF is used when we need to split or arrange things into the largest equal groups, such as finding the maximum number of columns for two groups to march, or the largest tile size to cover a floor. LCM is used when events repeat at different intervals and we want to know when they happen together, such as when traffic lights or bells coincide, or when runners meet at a starting point. Whether to use HCF or LCM depends on whether we are grouping/splitting (HCF) or synchronising repeated cycles (LCM). These applications rely directly on the Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic and the methods of finding HCF and LCM.
topics.easyExplanation: Use HCF to split things into largest equal groups, and LCM to find when repeating events happen at the same time.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Read the problem
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Decide whether you are grouping items or synchronising repeating events.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Choose HCF or LCM
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Largest equal groups means HCF; events coinciding means LCM.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Factorise the numbers
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Write each given number in prime factorised form.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Compute the answer
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Apply HCF (lowest common powers) or LCM (highest powers).

stepByStepExplanation.title: Interpret in context
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: State the answer in the real-life terms of the question.

examples: Two bells ring every 12 and 18 minutes; they ring together after LCM(12,18)=36 minutes.
Largest tile to exactly cover a floor 6 m by 9 m: side = HCF(600,900)=300 cm = 3 m.

importantKeywords.definition: To make events line up at the same time.
Arranging items into equal sets.
The gap between repeated events.

topics.quickSummary: HCF and LCM solve practical problems: HCF for largest equal grouping or tiling, LCM for when repeating cycles coincide. Choosing correctly depends on whether the task is splitting items or synchronising events.
revisionNotes: HCF for largest equal groups or tiles.
LCM for when repeating events coincide.

topics.meaning: A polynomial in x is an expression of the form a_n x^n + ... + a_1 x + a_0, and its degree is the highest power of x with non-zero coefficient.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: A polynomial p(x) is an algebraic expression in which the powers of the variable are whole numbers, written as a_n x^n + a_(n-1) x^(n-1) + ... + a_1 x + a_0. The degree of a polynomial is the highest power of the variable having a non-zero coefficient. A polynomial of degree 1 is called linear (e.g. 2x - 3), degree 2 is quadratic (e.g. ax^2 + bx + c with a not 0), and degree 3 is cubic (e.g. ax^3 + bx^2 + cx + d). Expressions with negative or fractional powers of the variable are not polynomials. The coefficients are real numbers. Understanding degree helps predict the maximum number of zeroes a polynomial can have.
topics.easyExplanation: A polynomial is a sum of terms with whole-number powers of x. Its degree is the biggest power. Degree 1 is linear, 2 is quadratic, 3 is cubic.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Identify the variable terms
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Look at the expression, e.g. 5x^3 - 4x^2 + x - 2.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Find the highest power
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The largest exponent of x here is 3.

stepByStepExplanation.title: State the degree
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: So the degree of the polynomial is 3.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Name by degree
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Degree 3 means it is a cubic polynomial.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Check validity
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Ensure all powers are whole numbers; sqrt(x) or 1/x would make it not a polynomial.

examples: The polynomial 2y^2 - 3y + 4 has degree 2, so it is quadratic.
The expression 7u^6 - (3/2)u^4 + 4u^2 + u - 8 has degree 6.

importantKeywords.definition: The highest power of the variable in a polynomial.
The number multiplying a power of the variable.
A polynomial of degree 1.

topics.quickSummary: A polynomial has whole-number powers of its variable, and its degree is the highest such power. Degrees 1, 2, 3 give linear, quadratic and cubic polynomials, and degree limits the number of zeroes.
revisionNotes: No negative or fractional powers allowed.

topics.meaning: A zero of a polynomial p(x) is a value k of the variable for which p(k) = 0.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: A real number k is called a zero of a polynomial p(x) if p(k) = 0, meaning substituting k makes the polynomial equal to zero. For example, for p(x) = x^2 - 3x - 4, both -1 and 4 are zeroes because p(-1) = 0 and p(4) = 0. To find the value of a polynomial at a number, substitute that number for the variable and simplify. The zero of a linear polynomial ax + b is x = -b/a. A polynomial of degree n has at most n zeroes. Zeroes are also called roots when the polynomial is set equal to zero.
topics.easyExplanation: A zero is a value of x that makes the polynomial equal zero. For example, x = 4 is a zero of x^2 - 3x - 4 because it gives 0.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Set the polynomial
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Take p(x), for example x^2 - 3x - 4.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Substitute a value
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Replace x with the candidate, e.g. x = 4: 16 - 12 - 4.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Simplify
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Compute the result: 16 - 12 - 4 = 0.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Conclude zero
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Since p(4) = 0, the number 4 is a zero of p(x).

stepByStepExplanation.title: Use the linear formula
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: For ax + b, the zero is x = -b/a; e.g. 2x + 3 has zero -3/2.

examples: Find the zero of 2x + 3: set 2x + 3 = 0 so x = -3/2.
Check if 2 is a zero of x^2 - 5x + 6: 4 - 10 + 6 = 0, yes 2 is a zero.

importantKeywords.definition: A value of the variable making the polynomial zero.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: x=4 is a zero of x^2-3x-4.

importantKeywords.definition: A solution of the equation p(x)=0.
Replacing the variable with a number.

topics.quickSummary: A zero of p(x) is a value k with p(k) = 0; for a linear polynomial ax + b it is -b/a. A degree n polynomial has at most n zeroes, found by substitution or solving p(x)=0.
revisionNotes: Degree n => at most n zeroes.

topics.meaning: The zeroes of a polynomial are the x-coordinates of the points where the graph of y = p(x) crosses or touches the x-axis.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Geometrically, the zeroes of a polynomial p(x) are exactly the x-coordinates of the points where the graph y = p(x) meets the x-axis. A linear polynomial ax + b gives a straight line that crosses the x-axis at exactly one point, so it has one zero. A quadratic polynomial ax^2 + bx + c gives a parabola (open upward if a > 0, downward if a < 0) that can meet the x-axis at two points (two zeroes), touch it at one point (one zero), or not meet it at all (no real zeroes). A cubic polynomial can cross the x-axis at up to three points. In general a degree n polynomial meets the x-axis at most n times.
topics.easyExplanation: Draw the graph of the polynomial. Wherever it touches or crosses the x-axis gives a zero. A parabola can cross twice, touch once, or miss the axis.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Plot the graph
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Sketch y = p(x), e.g. the parabola for x^2 - 3x - 4.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Find x-axis crossings
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Note where the curve meets the x-axis.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Read x-coordinates
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Those x-values are the zeroes, here -1 and 4.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Count the zeroes
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Number of crossings/touches gives the number of real zeroes.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Relate to degree
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: A parabola meets x-axis in at most 2 points; degree n in at most n points.

examples: The parabola y = x^2 - 3x - 4 cuts the x-axis at x = -1 and x = 4, its two zeroes.
The parabola y = x^2 + 1 never meets the x-axis, so it has no real zeroes.

importantKeywords.definition: The U-shaped graph of a quadratic polynomial.
The horizontal axis where y = 0.
A point where the graph meets an axis.

topics.quickSummary: Zeroes are the x-coordinates where the graph of y = p(x) meets the x-axis. A line gives one zero, a parabola gives two, one, or no real zeroes, and a degree n curve meets the x-axis at most n times.
revisionNotes: Parabola: two, one, or no real zeroes.
Degree n => at most n crossings.

topics.meaning: For a quadratic ax^2 + bx + c with zeroes alpha and beta, the sum of zeroes is -b/a and the product is c/a.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: For a quadratic polynomial ax^2 + bx + c (a not 0) with zeroes alpha and beta, there is a direct relationship with the coefficients: the sum of the zeroes alpha + beta = -b/a = -(coefficient of x)/(coefficient of x^2), and the product of the zeroes alpha x beta = c/a = (constant term)/(coefficient of x^2). These relations let you find the sum and product without computing the zeroes, and conversely, if the sum (S) and product (P) of zeroes are known, the quadratic can be reconstructed as k[x^2 - Sx + P] for any non-zero constant k. This connection is used widely in forming and verifying quadratic polynomials.
topics.easyExplanation: For ax^2 + bx + c, the two zeroes add up to -b/a and multiply to c/a. You can build a quadratic from its sum and product as x^2 - (sum)x + (product).

stepByStepExplanation.title: Identify a, b, c
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: From the quadratic, e.g. 2x^2 - 8x + 6, take a=2, b=-8, c=6.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Compute sum
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Sum of zeroes = -b/a = -(-8)/2 = 4.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Compute product
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Product of zeroes = c/a = 6/2 = 3.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Verify with zeroes
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Factorise: zeroes are 1 and 3; 1+3=4 and 1 x 3=3 match.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Build from sum and product
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: To form a quadratic with sum S and product P use x^2 - Sx + P.

examples: For x^2 - 2x - 8: sum = -(-2)/1 = 2, product = -8/1 = -8; zeroes 4 and -2 confirm this.
A quadratic with sum 3 and product 2 is x^2 - 3x + 2, whose zeroes are 1 and 2.

importantKeywords.definition: alpha + beta = -b/a for ax^2+bx+c.
alpha x beta = c/a.
The term without a variable.

topics.quickSummary: For a quadratic ax^2 + bx + c, the sum of zeroes is -b/a and the product is c/a. These let you find or verify zeroes and reconstruct a quadratic as x^2 - (sum)x + (product).

topics.topic: Forming a Quadratic from Its Zeroes
topics.meaning: A quadratic polynomial can be constructed from its zeroes using k[x^2 - (sum of zeroes)x + (product of zeroes)].
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: If the zeroes of a required quadratic polynomial are known, or their sum and product are given, the polynomial can be formed using the formula k[x^2 - (alpha + beta)x + alpha x beta], where k is any non-zero real number (usually taken as 1). Here alpha + beta is the sum of the zeroes and alpha x beta is their product. For example, a quadratic with zeroes 3 and -2 has sum 1 and product -6, giving x^2 - x - 6. This is the reverse of finding zeroes from a polynomial and uses the same coefficient relationships. Different values of k give polynomials with the same zeroes but scaled coefficients.
topics.easyExplanation: To build a quadratic, use x^2 - (sum of zeroes)x + (product of zeroes). For zeroes 3 and -2, you get x^2 - x - 6.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Find the two zeroes
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Identify alpha and beta, e.g. 3 and -2.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Compute their sum
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: alpha + beta = 3 + (-2) = 1.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Compute their product
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: alpha x beta = 3 x (-2) = -6.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Substitute into formula
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Form x^2 - (sum)x + (product) = x^2 - 1x + (-6).

stepByStepExplanation.title: Write the polynomial
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The quadratic is x^2 - x - 6 (taking k = 1).

examples: Zeroes -3 and 5: sum = 2, product = -15, polynomial x^2 - 2x - 15.
Zeroes with sum -1/4 and product 1/4: polynomial 4x^2 + x + 1 (after clearing fractions).

importantKeywords.definition: Building a polynomial back from its zeroes.
The factor k that scales coefficients.
The two quantities that define the quadratic.

topics.quickSummary: A quadratic is formed from its zeroes as k[x^2 - (sum)x + (product)]. Compute sum and product of the given zeroes, substitute, and choose k (usually 1) to get the polynomial.
revisionNotes: S = sum of zeroes, P = product.

topics.meaning: Verifying involves checking that computed zeroes satisfy both the polynomial equals zero and the sum/product relations with the coefficients.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: After finding the zeroes of a quadratic, it is good practice to verify them in two ways. First, substitute each zero back into the polynomial to confirm p(zero) = 0. Second, check that the sum of the zeroes equals -b/a and the product equals c/a, where a, b, c are the coefficients. For instance, for 2x^2 - 8x + 6 the zeroes are 1 and 3: substituting gives 0 each time, and the sum 4 = -(-8)/2 while the product 3 = 6/2. This double-checking confirms the answer is correct and reinforces the link between zeroes and coefficients. It is especially useful in examinations to catch arithmetic errors.
topics.easyExplanation: Check your zeroes by putting them back into the polynomial (should give 0) and by confirming their sum is -b/a and product is c/a.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Find the zeroes
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Solve the quadratic, e.g. 2x^2 - 8x + 6 gives zeroes 1 and 3.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Substitute each zero
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Put x = 1 and x = 3 into the polynomial; both give 0.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Check the sum
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Sum 1 + 3 = 4 should equal -b/a = -(-8)/2 = 4.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Check the product
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Product 1 x 3 = 3 should equal c/a = 6/2 = 3.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Confirm correctness
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Since all checks match, the zeroes are verified.

examples: For x^2 - 7x + 10, zeroes 2 and 5: 2+5=7=-(-7)/1 and 2 x 5=10=10/1, verified.
For 4x^2 - 4x + 1, zero 1/2 (double): sum 1 = -(-4)/4, product 1/4 = 1/4, verified.

importantKeywords.definition: Confirming a result by independent checks.
Replacing the variable with the zero value.
Agreement between two methods.

topics.quickSummary: Verify zeroes by substituting them to get zero and by checking sum = -b/a and product = c/a. Matching results confirm correctness and reinforce the zero-coefficient relationship.

topics.meaning: A cubic polynomial has degree 3 and can have up to three zeroes, which relate to its coefficients through sum, sum of pair products, and product.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: A cubic polynomial has the form ax^3 + bx^2 + cx + d with a not 0 and degree 3, so it can have at most three zeroes. If its three zeroes are alpha, beta and gamma, then there are coefficient relationships: alpha + beta + gamma = -b/a, alpha x beta + beta x gamma + gamma x alpha = c/a, and alpha x beta x gamma = -d/a. Geometrically the graph of a cubic crosses the x-axis at up to three points. These relationships generalise those of the quadratic and allow verification of zeroes or finding an unknown zero when others are known. Cubic factorisation often uses one known zero to reduce to a quadratic.
topics.easyExplanation: A cubic has degree 3 and up to three zeroes. Their sum is -b/a, the sum of products in pairs is c/a, and their product is -d/a.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Write the cubic
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Take ax^3 + bx^2 + cx + d, e.g. x^3 - 6x^2 + 11x - 6.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Sum of zeroes
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: alpha+beta+gamma = -b/a = 6.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Sum of pair products
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: alpha x beta + beta x gamma + gamma x alpha = c/a = 11.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Product of zeroes
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: alpha x beta x gamma = -d/a = 6; zeroes 1,2,3 satisfy all three.

examples: For x^3 - 6x^2 + 11x - 6, zeroes are 1, 2, 3: sum 6, pair-sum 11, product 6.
If a cubic 2x^3 + x^2 - 5x + 2 has zeroes summing to -b/a = -1/2, this checks the coefficient.

importantKeywords.definition: A polynomial of degree 3.
Product of zeroes taken two at a time.
Product of all three zeroes.

topics.quickSummary: A cubic ax^3 + bx^2 + cx + d has up to three zeroes with sum -b/a, pair-product sum c/a, and product -d/a. These relations generalise the quadratic case and aid verification and factorisation.
revisionNotes: Cubic has degree 3, up to 3 zeroes.

topics.meaning: Zeroes of a quadratic can be found by splitting the middle term to factorise it into two linear factors and setting each to zero.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: A common method to find zeroes of a quadratic ax^2 + bx + c is factorisation by splitting the middle term. We split the middle coefficient b into two numbers whose sum is b and whose product is a x c. Then we group terms, take out common factors, and write the quadratic as a product of two linear factors. Setting each factor to zero gives the zeroes. For example, 2x^2 - 8x + 6: here a x c = 12 and we split -8 as -2 and -6 (sum -8, product 12). Factorising gives (x - 1)(2x - 6) = 0, so the zeroes are 1 and 3. This method works whenever the quadratic factorises over rationals.
topics.easyExplanation: Split the middle term into two parts whose product is a times c, factorise by grouping, then set each factor to zero to get the zeroes.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Split the middle term
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Find two numbers with product 12 and sum -8: these are -2 and -6.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Rewrite and group
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Write 2x^2 - 2x - 6x + 6 and group as 2x(x-1) - 6(x-1).

stepByStepExplanation.title: Factorise
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Take out (x-1): (x-1)(2x-6).

stepByStepExplanation.title: Set factors to zero
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: x - 1 = 0 gives 1; 2x - 6 = 0 gives 3; zeroes are 1 and 3.

examples: Factorise x^2 - 5x + 6: split as -2,-3, giving (x-2)(x-3); zeroes 2 and 3.
Factorise 6x^2 - 7x - 3: a x c=-18, split -9 and 2, giving (3x+1)(2x-3); zeroes -1/3 and 3/2.

importantKeywords.definition: Breaking b into two parts summing to b with product ac.
Writing as a product of factors.
Pairing terms to extract common factors.

topics.quickSummary: To find quadratic zeroes by factorisation, split the middle term into two numbers with sum b and product a x c, group and factorise into linear factors, then set each to zero. The factor values are the zeroes.
revisionNotes: Split middle term: sum b, product ac.
Each gives a zero of the quadratic.

chapters.chapter: Pair of Linear Equations in Two Variables

topics.topic: Pair of Linear Equations in Two Variables
topics.meaning: A pair of linear equations in two variables consists of two equations of the form ax + by + c = 0, considered together for common solutions.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: A linear equation in two variables x and y has the general form ax + by + c = 0, where a, b, c are real numbers and a, b are not both zero. A pair of such equations, a1x + b1y + c1 = 0 and a2x + b2y + c2 = 0, is studied together to find values of x and y that satisfy both simultaneously. Such a pair is also called a system of simultaneous linear equations. A solution is an ordered pair (x, y) making both equations true. These arise in real-life situations such as comparing costs, ages, or mixtures, where two conditions must hold at once. The pair can be solved graphically or algebraically.
topics.easyExplanation: Two linear equations in x and y considered together form a pair. A solution is a pair of values that fits both equations at the same time.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Write both equations
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Express each in the form ax + by + c = 0, e.g. 2x + 3y - 9 = 0 and 4x + 6y - 18 = 0.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Note the coefficients
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Identify a1, b1, c1 and a2, b2, c2 for each equation.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Understand a solution
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: A solution (x, y) must satisfy both equations together.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Choose a method
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Decide to solve graphically or algebraically.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Interpret
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Relate the solution back to the real-life situation if given.

examples: The pair x + y = 14 and x - y = 4 has the solution x = 9, y = 5.
2 pencils and 3 erasers cost 9 units: 2x + 3y = 9 forms one equation of a pair.

importantKeywords.definition: An equation of degree one in the variables.
Two or more equations considered together.
Values satisfying all equations simultaneously.

topics.quickSummary: A pair of linear equations in two variables is two equations ax + by + c = 0 solved together. A solution is an (x, y) pair satisfying both, found graphically or algebraically, modelling real two-condition problems.

topics.meaning: The graphical method solves a pair of linear equations by drawing both lines and finding their point of intersection.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: In the graphical method, each linear equation is represented by a straight line on the coordinate plane. The solution of the pair is the point where the two lines intersect, because that point satisfies both equations. Three cases arise: if the lines intersect at one point, there is a unique solution (consistent system); if the lines coincide (are the same), there are infinitely many solutions (dependent and consistent); if the lines are parallel and distinct, there is no solution (inconsistent system). To draw each line, find at least two points by choosing values of x and computing y. The nature of solution can be predicted from the coefficients before plotting.
topics.easyExplanation: Draw both equations as lines. Where they cross is the solution. Parallel lines mean no solution; the same line means infinitely many solutions.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Make tables of points
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: For each equation pick x-values and compute y to get two or more points.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Plot the lines
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Plot the points and draw the straight line for each equation.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Observe the lines
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: See whether the lines intersect, coincide, or are parallel.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Find intersection
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: If they cross at one point, read its coordinates as the unique solution.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Classify the system
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Coinciding lines give infinitely many solutions; parallel lines give no solution.

examples: x + y = 4 and x - y = 2 cross at (3,1), the unique solution.
2x + 3y = 9 and 4x + 6y = 18 are the same line, giving infinitely many solutions.

importantKeywords.definition: Lines meeting at exactly one point.
Two equations representing the same line.
Lines that never meet.

topics.quickSummary: The graphical method plots both equations as lines; their intersection is the solution. One crossing means a unique solution, coinciding lines mean infinitely many, and distinct parallel lines mean none.
revisionNotes: Solution = intersection of the two lines.

topics.meaning: The ratios of coefficients a1/a2, b1/b2, c1/c2 determine whether a pair of linear equations has a unique, infinitely many, or no solution.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: For a pair a1x + b1y + c1 = 0 and a2x + b2y + c2 = 0, comparing the ratios of coefficients reveals the nature of the solution without drawing graphs. If a1/a2 is not equal to b1/b2, the lines intersect at one point and the system has a unique solution (consistent). If a1/a2 = b1/b2 = c1/c2, the lines coincide and there are infinitely many solutions (consistent and dependent). If a1/a2 = b1/b2 but not equal to c1/c2, the lines are parallel and there is no solution (inconsistent). This algebraic test is quick and reliable for classifying any pair of linear equations.
topics.easyExplanation: Compare the ratios of the coefficients. Unequal x and y ratios give one solution; all three equal give infinitely many; equal x,y ratios but different constant gives no solution.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Write in standard form
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Arrange both equations as ax + by + c = 0.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Form the ratios
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Compute a1/a2, b1/b2 and c1/c2.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Test for unique
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: If a1/a2 is not equal to b1/b2, there is a unique solution.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Test for infinite
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: If a1/a2 = b1/b2 = c1/c2, there are infinitely many solutions.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Test for none
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: If a1/a2 = b1/b2 but not equal to c1/c2, there is no solution.

examples: x - 2y = 0 and 3x + 4y = 20: 1/3 not equal to -2/4, so unique solution exists.
2x + 3y = 9 and 4x + 6y = 18: 2/4 = 3/6 = 9/18, so infinitely many solutions.

importantKeywords.definition: A system having at least one solution.
A system having no solution.
Equations representing the same line.

topics.quickSummary: Comparing a1/a2, b1/b2, c1/c2 classifies a pair: unequal x,y ratios give a unique solution, all three equal give infinitely many, and equal x,y ratios with different constant give none.

topics.meaning: The substitution method solves a pair of equations by expressing one variable in terms of the other and substituting into the second equation.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The substitution method is an algebraic technique to solve a pair of linear equations. First, one equation is solved to express one variable (say y) in terms of the other (x). This expression is then substituted into the second equation, producing a single equation in one variable, which is solved. The found value is substituted back to get the second variable. For example, from x + y = 14 we get y = 14 - x, which substituted into x - y = 4 gives x - (14 - x) = 4, so x = 9 and then y = 5. The method is most convenient when one variable has coefficient 1 and is easy to isolate.
topics.easyExplanation: Solve one equation for one variable, put that into the other equation to get one variable, solve it, then find the other variable.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Isolate a variable
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: From one equation express a variable, e.g. from x + y = 14 write y = 14 - x.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Substitute
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Put this into the other equation: x - (14 - x) = 4.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Solve single variable
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Simplify to 2x - 14 = 4, so x = 9.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Back-substitute
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Put x = 9 into y = 14 - x to get y = 5.

stepByStepExplanation.title: State the solution
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The solution is x = 9, y = 5; check it in both equations.

examples: Solve s - t = 3 and s/3 + t/2 = 6: from first s = t + 3; substituting gives t = 6, s = 9.
Solve 2x + 3y = 11 and x = 2y - 1: substitute to get 2(2y-1)+3y=11, y=13/7.

importantKeywords.definition: Replacing a variable with an equivalent expression.
Getting a variable alone on one side.
Putting a found value back to get the other variable.

topics.quickSummary: The substitution method isolates one variable from an equation, substitutes it into the other to solve for one unknown, then back-substitutes for the second. It is easiest when a coefficient is 1.
revisionNotes: Express one variable from one equation.
Solve for one variable, then back-substitute.

topics.meaning: The elimination method solves a pair of equations by making the coefficients of one variable equal and adding or subtracting to eliminate it.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The elimination method removes one variable by combining the two equations. First, multiply one or both equations by suitable numbers so that the coefficients of one variable become equal in magnitude. Then add or subtract the equations to eliminate that variable, leaving a single equation in the other variable, which is solved. The value found is substituted back to get the eliminated variable. For example, to solve 2x + 3y = 8 and 4x + 9y = 22, multiply the first by 2 to get 4x + 6y = 16, then subtract from the second to eliminate x, giving 3y = 6 so y = 2 and then x = 1. This method is efficient when coefficients are easy to match.
topics.easyExplanation: Make the coefficient of one variable the same in both equations, then add or subtract to cancel it. Solve the remaining single-variable equation.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Match a coefficient
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Multiply equations so one variable has equal coefficients, e.g. make x-coefficients both 4.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Add or subtract
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Combine the equations to eliminate that variable.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Solve for one variable
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Solve the resulting single-variable equation, e.g. 3y = 6 gives y = 2.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Back-substitute
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Substitute y = 2 into an original equation to find x = 1.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Verify
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Check the solution (1, 2) satisfies both original equations.

examples: Solve x + y = 5 and 2x - y = 4: add to get 3x = 9, x = 3, then y = 2.
Solve 3x + 4y = 10 and 2x - 2y = 2: double second to 4x - 4y = 4, add to first, 7x = 14, x = 2, y = 1.

importantKeywords.definition: Removing one variable by combining equations.
Scaling equations to equalise a coefficient.
Combining equations term by term.

topics.quickSummary: The elimination method scales equations to equalise one variable's coefficient, then adds or subtracts to remove it, solving the remaining equation and back-substituting. It is efficient with easily matched coefficients.
revisionNotes: Add or subtract to eliminate it.

topics.meaning: Word problems are solved by translating the given conditions into a pair of linear equations and then solving them algebraically.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Many real-life situations can be modelled by a pair of linear equations in two variables. The key skill is translation: choose two unknowns, then write two equations from the two conditions in the problem. Common contexts include ages, costs of items, fractions, speeds (boat in stream), and two-digit numbers. Once the equations are formed, any algebraic method (substitution or elimination) solves them. The solution is then interpreted back in the words of the problem, and checked for reasonableness (for example, ages must be positive). Careful reading and clear definition of variables are essential to forming correct equations.
topics.easyExplanation: Turn the words into two equations using two unknowns, solve them, and interpret the answer in the context of the problem.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Define variables
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Let the two unknowns be x and y, e.g. the two digits or two ages.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Translate conditions
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Write one equation per condition given in the problem.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Choose a method
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Solve the pair using substitution or elimination.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Solve
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Find the values of x and y.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Interpret and check
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: State the answer in words and verify it makes sense and fits both conditions.

examples: Sum of two numbers is 26 and difference is 8: x + y = 26, x - y = 8, giving x = 17, y = 9.
A two-digit number with digit sum 9 and reversed number 27 less: x + y = 9 and 9(x - y) = 27 give the number.

importantKeywords.definition: Converting words into equations.
Clearly stating what x and y represent.
Explaining the solution in context.

topics.quickSummary: Word problems are solved by defining two variables, translating each condition into an equation, solving the pair algebraically, and interpreting and checking the result against the original situation.

topics.meaning: Some non-linear equations can be made linear by substituting a new variable for a reciprocal or similar expression.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Certain pairs of equations are not linear as given but can be reduced to linear form by a suitable substitution. For example, equations containing terms like 1/x and 1/y can be made linear by letting p = 1/x and q = 1/y, turning them into a linear pair in p and q. After solving for p and q, we recover x and y by taking reciprocals. For instance, 2/x + 3/y = 13 and 5/x - 4/y = -2 become 2p + 3q = 13 and 5p - 4q = -2 with p = 1/x, q = 1/y; solving gives p, q and then x, y. This technique extends the linear-equation methods to a wider class of problems.
topics.easyExplanation: Replace awkward parts like 1/x and 1/y with new letters p and q to make the equations linear, solve, then convert back to x and y.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Spot the pattern
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Notice terms like 1/x and 1/y in both equations.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Substitute
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Let p = 1/x and q = 1/y to get a linear pair in p and q.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Solve the linear pair
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Use substitution or elimination to find p and q.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Convert back
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Use x = 1/p and y = 1/q to recover the original variables.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Verify
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Substitute x and y back into the original equations to check.

examples: 1/(x+y) + 1/(x-y) = ... type problems become linear in p=1/(x+y), q=1/(x-y).

importantKeywords.definition: A new letter replacing an expression.
One divided by a quantity.
An equation made linear by substitution.

topics.quickSummary: Equations with reciprocal terms become linear after substituting new variables like p = 1/x and q = 1/y. Solve the resulting linear pair, then take reciprocals to find the original unknowns.

topics.meaning: Algebraic methods give exact solutions efficiently, while the graphical method gives a visual picture of the nature of the solution.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Both algebraic and graphical approaches solve a pair of linear equations, but they have different strengths. The graphical method shows the geometry: whether lines intersect, coincide, or are parallel, giving a clear visual of unique, infinite, or no solution. However, it may be imprecise when the solution is not at integer coordinates. Algebraic methods (substitution, elimination) give exact answers quickly and work for any coefficients. The coefficient-ratio test predicts the nature of the solution instantly. In practice, the graphical method aids understanding while algebraic methods are preferred for accuracy. Choosing between substitution and elimination depends on which makes the arithmetic simpler.
topics.easyExplanation: Graphs show what kind of solution exists but can be imprecise; algebra gives exact answers. Use graphs to understand and algebra to compute accurately.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Decide the goal
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Determine whether you need a visual understanding or an exact value.

stepByStepExplanation.title: For visual insight
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Use the graphical method to see intersecting, coincident, or parallel lines.

stepByStepExplanation.title: For exact values
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Use substitution or elimination to compute precise solutions.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Predict nature quickly
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Apply the coefficient-ratio test for the type of solution.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Pick easiest algebra
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Choose substitution when a coefficient is 1, else elimination.

examples: Graphically, x+y=4 and x-y=2 clearly meet at (3,1); algebra confirms x=3, y=1 exactly.
For non-integer solutions like x=13/7, the algebraic method is more reliable than reading a graph.

importantKeywords.definition: Solving by drawing and reading line intersections.
Solving by substitution or elimination.
Exactness of the computed solution.

topics.quickSummary: Graphical solutions reveal the nature of the system visually but can lack precision; algebraic methods give exact answers efficiently. The ratio test quickly predicts the type, and the simpler algebra is chosen per problem.
revisionNotes: Ratio test predicts the nature fast.
Choose substitution or elimination by ease.

topics.topic: Standard Form of a Quadratic Equation
topics.meaning: A quadratic equation is an equation of the form ax^2 + bx + c = 0 where a, b, c are real numbers and a is not zero.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: A quadratic equation in the variable x is any equation that can be written in the standard form ax^2 + bx + c = 0, where a, b, c are real numbers and a is not equal to 0. The condition a not 0 ensures the equation is genuinely of degree two. Many real-life and geometric situations, such as areas and product-of-numbers problems, lead to quadratic equations. Any equation that, after simplification and rearrangement, takes this form is quadratic. For example, (x + 1)(x - 2) = 0 expands to x^2 - x - 2 = 0. Recognising and writing the standard form is the first step before solving.
topics.easyExplanation: A quadratic equation looks like ax^2 + bx + c = 0 with a not zero. The highest power of x is 2.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Examine the equation
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Look at the given equation, e.g. (x+1)(x-2)=0.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Expand and simplify
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Multiply out to get x^2 - x - 2 = 0.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Arrange to one side
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Move all terms to one side so it equals 0.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Confirm a not 0
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Since a = 1 is non-zero, it is a valid quadratic equation.

examples: Express (x-3)(x+2) = 0 in standard form: x^2 - x - 6 = 0, with a=1, b=-1, c=-6.
2x^2 = 5x - 3 rearranges to 2x^2 - 5x + 3 = 0.

importantKeywords.definition: An equation of degree two in one variable.
ax^2 + bx + c = 0 with a not 0.
The number multiplying x^2.

topics.quickSummary: A quadratic equation has the standard form ax^2 + bx + c = 0 with a not 0. Recognising and rearranging equations into this form is the necessary first step before applying any solution method.
revisionNotes: Rearrange all terms to one side.

topics.meaning: A root (or solution) of a quadratic equation is a value of the variable that makes the equation equal to zero.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: A real number alpha is called a root or solution of the quadratic equation ax^2 + bx + c = 0 if substituting x = alpha makes the left side equal to zero, that is a(alpha)^2 + b(alpha) + c = 0. The roots of the quadratic equation are the same as the zeroes of the corresponding quadratic polynomial ax^2 + bx + c. A quadratic equation can have at most two roots. Checking whether a number is a root simply requires substitution. Finding the roots is the central goal, achieved by factorisation or the quadratic formula. The roots represent the solutions to whatever problem the equation models.
topics.easyExplanation: A root is a value of x that makes the quadratic equal zero. A quadratic has at most two roots, and you can test a value by substituting it.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Take the equation
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Consider ax^2 + bx + c = 0, e.g. x^2 - 5x + 6 = 0.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Pick a candidate
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Choose a value to test, say x = 2.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Decide
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Since the result is 0, x = 2 is a root.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Note the count
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: A quadratic has at most two roots; here the other is x = 3.

examples: Check x = 3 in x^2 - 5x + 6 = 0: 9 - 15 + 6 = 0, so 3 is a root.
Is x = 1 a root of 2x^2 - 3x + 1 = 0? 2 - 3 + 1 = 0, yes it is.

importantKeywords.definition: A value satisfying the equation = 0.
Another name for a root.
Testing a value by plugging it in.

topics.quickSummary: A root of a quadratic equation is a value making it zero, identical to a zero of the corresponding polynomial. A quadratic has at most two roots, and any candidate is verified by substitution.
revisionNotes: At most two roots per quadratic.

topics.meaning: Factorisation solves a quadratic by writing it as a product of two linear factors and setting each factor to zero.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Solving a quadratic equation ax^2 + bx + c = 0 by factorisation uses the splitting of the middle term. We find two numbers whose product is a x c and whose sum is b, split the middle term accordingly, group, and factorise into two linear factors. By the zero-product rule, if a product equals zero then at least one factor is zero, so we set each factor to zero and solve for x. For example, 2x^2 - 5x + 3 = 0 factorises as (2x - 3)(x - 1) = 0, giving x = 3/2 or x = 1. This method is quick when the quadratic factorises over the rationals.
topics.easyExplanation: Split the middle term, factorise the quadratic into two brackets, then set each bracket to zero to find the two roots.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Compute a times c
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: For 2x^2 - 5x + 3, a x c = 6.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Split the middle term
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Find numbers with product 6 and sum -5: -2 and -3.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Apply zero-product rule
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Set 2x - 3 = 0 and x - 1 = 0.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Solve each factor
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Roots are x = 3/2 and x = 1.

examples: Solve x^2 - 3x - 10 = 0: factorise (x-5)(x+2)=0, roots x=5 and x=-2.
Solve 6x^2 - x - 2 = 0: factorise (3x-2)(2x+1)=0, roots x=2/3 and x=-1/2.

importantKeywords.definition: Writing as a product of factors.
If a product is 0, a factor is 0.
The bx term of the quadratic.

topics.quickSummary: Factorisation solves a quadratic by splitting the middle term, factorising into two linear brackets, and using the zero-product rule to set each bracket to zero. The factor values give the roots.
revisionNotes: Split middle term: product ac, sum b.

topics.meaning: The quadratic formula x = (-b +/- sqrt(b^2 - 4ac))/(2a) gives the roots of any quadratic equation ax^2 + bx + c = 0.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: When a quadratic equation does not factorise easily, the quadratic formula provides the roots directly. For ax^2 + bx + c = 0 with a not 0, the roots are x = (-b +/- sqrt(b^2 - 4ac))/(2a). The expression b^2 - 4ac under the square root is called the discriminant. Real roots exist only when the discriminant is greater than or equal to 0. The formula is derived by the method of completing the square. To use it, identify a, b, c, compute the discriminant, then substitute into the formula. For example, for 3x^2 - 5x + 2 = 0, a=3, b=-5, c=2, discriminant = 25 - 24 = 1, giving roots 1 and 2/3.
topics.easyExplanation: Use x = (-b +/- sqrt(b^2 - 4ac))/(2a) to find the roots of any quadratic. Just plug in a, b and c.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Identify a, b, c
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: From the equation, e.g. 3x^2 - 5x + 2 = 0, take a=3, b=-5, c=2.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Check it is non-negative
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Since 1 >= 0, real roots exist.

examples: Solve 2x^2 + x - 6 = 0: discriminant = 1 + 48 = 49, x = (-1 +/- 7)/4, roots 3/2 and -2.
Solve x^2 - 4x + 1 = 0: discriminant = 16 - 4 = 12, x = (4 +/- 2 sqrt(3))/2 = 2 +/- sqrt(3).

importantKeywords.definition: The value b^2 - 4ac.
The radical part of the formula.

topics.quickSummary: The quadratic formula x = (-b +/- sqrt(b^2 - 4ac))/(2a) solves any quadratic. Identify a, b, c, compute the discriminant, and substitute; real roots require the discriminant to be non-negative.

topics.topic: The Discriminant and Nature of Roots
topics.meaning: The discriminant b^2 - 4ac determines whether a quadratic equation has two distinct real, two equal real, or no real roots.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The nature of the roots of ax^2 + bx + c = 0 is decided by the discriminant D = b^2 - 4ac without solving the equation. If D > 0, the equation has two distinct real roots. If D = 0, it has two equal real roots (a repeated root), equal to -b/(2a). If D < 0, the equation has no real roots (the roots are not real). This is because the square root in the quadratic formula is real only when D is non-negative. Knowing the nature of roots in advance is useful for many problems, including conditions for equal roots which lead to relations among a, b, c.
topics.easyExplanation: Compute D = b^2 - 4ac. If D is positive there are two different real roots; if zero, two equal roots; if negative, no real roots.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Identify a, b, c
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: From the quadratic, e.g. 2x^2 - 4x + 3 = 0, take a=2, b=-4, c=3.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Compare with zero
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Check whether D is positive, zero, or negative.

stepByStepExplanation.title: State the nature
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Here D = -8 < 0, so there are no real roots.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Use special cases
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: For equal roots set D = 0 to find conditions on a, b, c.

examples: For x^2 - 5x + 6 = 0, D = 25 - 24 = 1 > 0, so two distinct real roots.
For x^2 - 4x + 4 = 0, D = 16 - 16 = 0, so two equal real roots, both x = 2.

importantKeywords.definition: D = b^2 - 4ac, deciding root nature.
Two different real solutions.
One repeated real solution.

topics.quickSummary: The discriminant D = b^2 - 4ac classifies roots: D > 0 gives two distinct real roots, D = 0 gives two equal real roots, and D < 0 gives no real roots. This predicts root nature without solving.

topics.meaning: Completing the square solves a quadratic by rewriting it as a perfect square equal to a constant, then taking square roots.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Completing the square is a method that converts ax^2 + bx + c = 0 into the form (x + p)^2 = q. First make the coefficient of x^2 equal to 1 by dividing through by a. Then add and subtract the square of half the coefficient of x to form a perfect square trinomial. The equation becomes (x + b/(2a))^2 = (b^2 - 4ac)/(4a^2). Taking square roots of both sides and solving gives the roots; in fact this derivation produces the quadratic formula. For example, x^2 + 4x - 5 = 0 becomes (x + 2)^2 = 9, so x + 2 = +/- 3, giving x = 1 or x = -5.
topics.easyExplanation: Rewrite the quadratic as a perfect square equal to a number, then take the square root of both sides to solve.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Make leading coefficient 1
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Divide through by a if needed, e.g. keep x^2 + 4x - 5 = 0.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Move constant across
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Write x^2 + 4x = 5.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Complete the square
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Add (half of 4)^2 = 4 to both sides: x^2 + 4x + 4 = 9.

examples: Solve 2x^2 - 4x - 6 = 0: divide by 2 to x^2 - 2x - 3 = 0, (x-1)^2 = 4, roots 3 and -1.

importantKeywords.definition: Forming a perfect square trinomial.
An expression that is a square of a binomial.
The coefficient of x^2.

topics.quickSummary: Completing the square rewrites a quadratic as (x + p)^2 = q by adding the square of half the x-coefficient, then solves by taking square roots. This method also derives the quadratic formula.
revisionNotes: Write as a perfect square = constant.

topics.meaning: A quadratic equation has two equal real roots exactly when its discriminant b^2 - 4ac equals zero.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: A quadratic ax^2 + bx + c = 0 has two equal (coincident) real roots if and only if its discriminant is zero, that is b^2 - 4ac = 0. In that case both roots equal -b/(2a). This condition is often used in problems where a parameter must be chosen so that the equation has equal roots, leading to an equation relating the coefficients. For example, to make x^2 - kx + 9 = 0 have equal roots, set k^2 - 36 = 0 so k = +/- 6. Geometrically, equal roots mean the corresponding parabola just touches the x-axis at one point. This condition is a frequent type of examination problem.
topics.easyExplanation: Equal roots happen when b^2 - 4ac = 0. Set the discriminant to zero to find unknown values that make a quadratic have equal roots.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Write the discriminant
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: For ax^2 + bx + c, the discriminant is b^2 - 4ac.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Set it to zero
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: For equal roots, require b^2 - 4ac = 0.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Form the condition
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: This gives an equation in the unknown parameter.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Solve for the parameter
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Solve, e.g. k^2 - 36 = 0 gives k = +/- 6.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Find the repeated root
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The equal root is -b/(2a).

examples: Find k so x^2 - kx + 9 = 0 has equal roots: k^2 - 36 = 0, k = +/- 6.
For 4x^2 + 4x + 1 = 0, discriminant = 16 - 16 = 0, so it has equal roots x = -1/2.

importantKeywords.definition: A root that occurs twice.
An unknown constant to be found.

topics.quickSummary: Equal roots occur exactly when the discriminant b^2 - 4ac = 0, with both roots equal to -b/(2a). Setting the discriminant to zero solves problems requiring a parameter for equal roots.

topics.meaning: Word problems are solved by translating real situations into a quadratic equation and selecting the meaningful root.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Many practical problems, involving areas, ages, speeds, numbers, and time, lead to quadratic equations. The method is to define a variable, translate the conditions into an equation, simplify it into standard quadratic form, and then solve by factorisation or the quadratic formula. A key step is rejecting any root that does not make sense in the context (for example, a negative length, age, or speed). For instance, if the product of two consecutive positive integers is 306, the equation x(x+1) = 306 gives x^2 + x - 306 = 0, with roots 17 and -18; only x = 17 is acceptable. Clear definition of the variable and checking validity are essential.
topics.easyExplanation: Turn the problem into a quadratic equation, solve it, and keep only the root that makes physical sense (positive lengths, ages, etc.).

stepByStepExplanation.title: Define the variable
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Let x stand for the unknown quantity, e.g. the smaller integer.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Form the equation
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Translate the condition, e.g. x(x+1) = 306.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Write standard form
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Rearrange to x^2 + x - 306 = 0.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Solve the quadratic
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Factorise or use the formula to get roots 17 and -18.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Select valid root
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Reject -18 (not positive); the integers are 17 and 18.

examples: Two consecutive positive integers with product 306: x^2 + x - 306 = 0, roots 17 and -18, answer 17 and 18.
A train's speed problem: x^2 + 5x - 750 = 0 gives x = 25 km/h after rejecting the negative root.

importantKeywords.definition: Forming an equation from a situation.
Discarding a meaningless solution.
Whole numbers one apart.

topics.quickSummary: Word problems become quadratic equations by defining a variable and translating conditions. After solving, only the root meaningful in context (positive length, age, speed) is accepted as the answer.
revisionNotes: Reject roots that make no sense.

topics.meaning: An arithmetic progression is a list of numbers in which each term is obtained by adding a fixed number, called the common difference, to the previous term.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: An arithmetic progression (AP) is a sequence of numbers a1, a2, a3, ... in which the difference between any two consecutive terms is constant. This constant is called the common difference, denoted d, and it can be positive, negative, or zero. The first term is denoted a. A general AP is written as a, a + d, a + 2d, a + 3d, and so on. For example, 2, 5, 8, 11, ... is an AP with a = 2 and d = 3. The common difference is found by subtracting any term from the term that follows it: d = a_(k+1) - a_k. APs model many real-life situations such as fixed annual salary increments and regular savings.
topics.easyExplanation: An AP is a list where you keep adding the same number each time. That fixed number is the common difference.

stepByStepExplanation.title: List the sequence
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Write down the given numbers, e.g. 2, 5, 8, 11.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Find consecutive differences
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Subtract each term from the next: 5-2=3, 8-5=3.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Check if constant
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: If all differences are equal, it is an AP.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Identify a and d
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The first term a = 2 and common difference d = 3.

examples: The list 3, 7, 11, 15 is an AP with a = 3 and d = 4.
Check if 1, 3, 9, 27 is an AP: differences 2, 6, 18 are not equal, so it is not an AP.

importantKeywords.definition: A sequence with constant difference between terms.
The fixed amount added each time, d.
The starting term a of the AP.

topics.quickSummary: An AP is a sequence where each term exceeds the previous by a constant common difference d, starting from first term a. It is written a, a+d, a+2d, ... and is recognised by equal consecutive differences.
revisionNotes: d = next term minus current term.
d can be positive, negative, or zero.

topics.meaning: The nth term of an AP with first term a and common difference d is given by a_n = a + (n - 1)d.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The general term, or nth term, of an arithmetic progression lets us find any term without listing all the earlier terms. If the first term is a and the common difference is d, then the nth term is a_n = a + (n - 1)d. Here n is the position of the term in the sequence. For example, in the AP 2, 7, 12, ... with a = 2 and d = 5, the 10th term is a_10 = 2 + (10 - 1) x 5 = 47. The same formula can be rearranged to find n, a, or d when the others are known. The last term of a finite AP with m terms is also denoted l = a + (m - 1)d.
topics.easyExplanation: To find any term of an AP use a_n = a + (n-1)d. Plug in the first term a, common difference d, and term number n.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Identify a and d
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: From the AP 2, 7, 12, take a = 2 and d = 5.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Write the formula
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Use a_n = a + (n - 1)d.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Substitute n
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: For the 10th term, n = 10: a_10 = 2 + (10-1) x 5.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Rearrange if needed
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: To find which term equals a value, solve a + (n-1)d = value for n.

examples: Find the 10th term of 2, 7, 12, ...: a_10 = 2 + 9 x 5 = 47.
Which term of 21, 18, 15, ... is -81? Solve 21 + (n-1)(-3) = -81 to get n = 35.

importantKeywords.definition: The term at position n, a + (n-1)d.
Another name for the nth term formula.
The final term l of a finite AP.

topics.quickSummary: The nth term of an AP is a_n = a + (n - 1)d, allowing any term to be found directly from a, d and n. The formula can also be rearranged to find n, a, d, or the last term l.
revisionNotes: Rearrange to find n, a, or d.

topics.topic: Sum of First n Terms of an AP
topics.meaning: The sum of the first n terms of an AP is S_n = (n/2)[2a + (n - 1)d], or equivalently (n/2)(a + l).
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The sum of the first n terms of an AP, denoted S_n, is found using S_n = (n/2)[2a + (n - 1)d], where a is the first term, d the common difference, and n the number of terms. This formula was derived using Gauss's pairing technique, adding the series to its reverse. When the first term a and last term l are known, the simpler form S_n = (n/2)(a + l) is used, since the last term l = a + (n - 1)d. For example, the sum of the first 21 terms of an AP with a = 100 and d = 50 is (21/2)[200 + 20 x 50] = 12600. The formula links the four quantities S, a, d, n.
topics.easyExplanation: To add the first n terms of an AP use S_n = (n/2)[2a + (n-1)d]. If you know the last term l, use S_n = (n/2)(a + l).

stepByStepExplanation.title: Identify a, d, n
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: From the AP take a, d, and the number of terms n.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Choose the formula
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Use (n/2)[2a + (n-1)d], or (n/2)(a + l) if l is known.

importantKeywords.definition: Total of the first n terms.
The nth term when used in the sum formula.
Adding the series to its reverse.

topics.quickSummary: The sum of n terms of an AP is S_n = (n/2)[2a + (n - 1)d], or (n/2)(a + l) when the last term is known. It connects S, a, d, and n, so any one can be found from the others.

topics.meaning: The nth term can be obtained from sums using a_n = S_n - S_(n-1), the difference of consecutive partial sums.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: There is a useful relationship between the sum of terms and the individual terms of an AP. The nth term equals the difference between the sum of the first n terms and the sum of the first (n-1) terms: a_n = S_n - S_(n-1). This is because S_n includes all terms up to the nth, while S_(n-1) includes all up to the (n-1)th, so their difference is exactly the nth term. This is especially handy when an AP is described by a formula for S_n rather than for its terms. For example, if S_n = 3n^2 + 5n, then a_n = S_n - S_(n-1) = 6n + 2, from which a and d can be read off.
topics.easyExplanation: To get the nth term when you know the sum formula, subtract the sum of (n-1) terms from the sum of n terms: a_n = S_n - S_(n-1).

stepByStepExplanation.title: Start with S_n
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Use the given sum formula, e.g. S_n = 3n^2 + 5n.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Write S_(n-1)
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Replace n by (n-1): 3(n-1)^2 + 5(n-1).

stepByStepExplanation.title: Simplify
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: This reduces to a_n = 6n + 2.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Read a and d
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: First term a_1 = 8 and d = 6 from the linear form.

examples: If S_n = 3n^2 + 5n, then a_n = 6n + 2, so a_1 = 8, d = 6.
If S_n = 4n - n^2, then a_n = S_n - S_(n-1) = 5 - 2n, giving a_1 = 3, d = -2.

importantKeywords.definition: Sum of the first n terms, S_n.
Subtracting consecutive sums.

topics.quickSummary: The nth term of an AP can be recovered from its sum formula using a_n = S_n - S_(n-1). This is useful when only the sum is given, allowing the first term and common difference to be found.
revisionNotes: Useful when S_n formula is given.
Result a_n is linear in n.
Read a and d from a_n form.

topics.topic: Common Difference and Checking an AP
topics.meaning: The common difference of an AP is found as d = a_(k+1) - a_k, and a sequence is an AP only if this difference is the same throughout.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The common difference d of an AP is the constant gap between consecutive terms, computed as d = a_(k+1) - a_k for any k. To check whether a given list of numbers forms an AP, compute the differences between several consecutive pairs; if they are all equal, it is an AP, otherwise it is not. The sign of d tells the behaviour: d > 0 gives an increasing AP, d < 0 a decreasing AP, and d = 0 a constant sequence. Knowing a and d completely determines the AP. This checking step is important before applying the nth-term or sum formulas, which only hold for genuine APs.
topics.easyExplanation: Find d by subtracting a term from the next. If every such difference is the same, the list is an AP.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Pick consecutive terms
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Take pairs of neighbouring terms from the list.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Compute differences
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Subtract each term from the one after it.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Compare differences
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Check whether all the differences are equal.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Decide
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: If equal, it is an AP with that common difference d.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Note the trend
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Positive d increases, negative d decreases, zero d stays constant.

examples: For 10, 7, 4, 1: differences are -3 each, so it is an AP with d = -3.
For 2, 4, 8, 16: differences 2, 4, 8 are unequal, so it is not an AP.

importantKeywords.definition: The constant d between consecutive terms.
An AP with positive common difference.
An AP with negative common difference.

topics.quickSummary: The common difference d = a_(k+1) - a_k must be constant for a list to be an AP. Its sign shows whether the AP increases, decreases, or stays constant, and a with d fully determines the AP.
revisionNotes: d = next term minus current term.
Constant d => it is an AP.

topics.meaning: AP word problems model real situations like salaries, savings, and seating that grow by a fixed amount, then apply AP formulas.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Arithmetic progressions describe many real-life patterns where a quantity changes by a constant amount, such as a salary rising by a fixed increment each year, regular yearly savings, the number of logs in stacked rows, or seats in a theatre with rows increasing steadily.
